result:
I want to write about the moment when the story you’re living stops feeling like a story at all.  
Not the cinematic beat when the orchestra swells and the audience knows the arc is complete, but the stranger, quieter collision that happens at 2:14 p.m. on a Tuesday so humid the air tastes like copper pennies, when you realize the narrative you’ve been rehearsing in your head has quietly liquified and run out through the cracks in the day.  
The plot you treasured—complete with charmingly flawed protagonist, foreshadowed redemption, and a moral you could recite like a catechism—suddenly refuses to cohere.  
What’s left is not dénouement, but compost: warm, stinking, alive.  
You are in it ankle-deep, and there is no authorial rescue forthcoming.

I keep thinking about the year my mother grew tomatoes on the fire escape.  
We lived on the fourth floor of a building that pretended to be brick but was really vinyl siding with delusions of grandeur.  
The tomatoes were started from seeds she saved out of a salad she ate in March, scraping the gelatinous embryos onto a paper towel the way other people might salvage a fortune-cookie prophecy.  
She planted them in recycled yogurt cups, labeled in eyeliner pencil because we couldn’t find a Sharpie.  
By June the plants resembled gangly adolescents, all elbows and acne, leaning dangerously over the railing into the Chicago alley.  
In August a storm came through and snapped the biggest stem; she duct-taped it like a cast and tied it to the wrought-iron railing with a shoelace.  
That plant produced nine golf-ball-sized tomatoes that tasted of acid and copper, and she ate them standing at the sink, sprinkling salt straight from the palm of her hand, crying without sound.  
I thought the scene meant something—resilience, maybe, or the persistence of life in a vertical city.  
But memory is a trick box: open it years later and the velvet lining has bled onto the jewels, staining them the color of bruise.  
I now suspect she was crying because the tomatoes were small and the rent was late and the future had begun to feel like a joke whose punch line she’d missed.  
The story I told myself then—indomitable widow wrestles sustenance from thin air—has decomposed into something richer and more frightening: a woman alone licking salt off imperfect fruit while traffic sighs below like an ocean that never quite arrives.

We are all unreliable narrators of our own lives, but the fraud is so gentle we rarely press charges.  
Take the photograph in my wallet: my father and me at Navy Pier, 1994.  
I am eight, wearing a neon-green mesh hat that says “Chicago’s Beach Bum.”  
He has one hand on my shoulder and one eye closed against the sun; the other eye is a wet hazel gemstone, looking somewhere left of the lens.  
For years I captioned this image “Last Summer Before the Drinking Got Bad,” a tidy inciting incident.  
Then, last winter, my aunt mailed me a packet of his medical records.  
Lab values yellowed like parchment, annotated in spidery nursing script.  
His liver was already cirrhotic by that August, platelets down to five digits, abdomen quietly flooding.  
The drinking got better for a while, not worse; the narrative inverted itself, a sock turned inside out.  
So the photograph no longer proves a beginning; it merely documents two people squinting against a moment that refused to announce its significance.  
I keep it anyway, the way you keep a ticket stub for a concert you only half-remember because the band played someone’s favorite song in the wrong key.

There is a kind of vertigo that arrives when the frame dissolves.  
You expect the world to behave like language: subject, verb, object, modifiers neatly appended.  
Instead you get a mouthful of ash and a view of the horizon that wobbles like gelatin.  
Philosophers have names for this—ontic shock, existential nausea, the burst balloon of ontological security—but the felt sense is closer to being dropped into deep water with your pockets full of subway tokens.  
You flail, certain you are drowning, until you notice the ocean is also inside you, saline matching saline, pulse matching moon.  
Then the panic becomes something else: a slow-motion baptism without clergy, a re-write that refuses the salvific logic of before-and-after.  
You surface, but you are not saved; you are porous, algae in your hair, tasting your own dissolved narratives.

I want to tell you about the night I drove to the shuttered amusement park.  
The gates were chained, but a section of fence lay supine in the weeds, so I stepped through like a trespasser in my own legend.  
The Tilt-A-Whirl cars sat in a mutinous circle, petals of faded pink and arsenic green.  
I walked the midway listening to cicadas rev their tiny engines, and I thought: this is what becomes of joy when no one is watching.  
Vines had braided through the roller-coaster tracks; the funhouse mirror maze reflected only moonlight and the occasional perplexed raccoon.  
I found the photo booth—curtain shredded, bulb exploded—and squeezed inside anyway, drew the tattered cloth, and spoke aloud the words I wished someone had spoken to me at thirteen:  
“You will not die from embarrassment.  
The cruel joke of your body will soften, then harden into something else, then soften again, like seasons misremembered.  
You will betray a friend and be betrayed, and both wounds will instruct you how to breathe underwater.  
One day you will stand in the ruins of a place designed for delight, and you will not require it to mean anything.”  
The booth did not flash; no strip of four black-and-white glossies emerged.  
Still, I felt photographed by the dark itself, an exposure so long it required absolute stillness.  
When I finally backed out, the gravel crunched like applause from an audience of constellations too ancient to care about plot.

Language, too, rots if left untended.  
I used to hoard pretty words the way squirrels hoard acorns, believing I could survive winter on shimmer alone.  
Then I met a man who spoke only in facts: torque specifications, bus schedules, the precise boiling point of mercury.  
We moved in together on the equinox, and by the solstice I had misplaced my adjectives.  
I would reach for “luminous” and come back with “sixty-watt.”  
My sentences grew calluses; they could lift cinder blocks without gloves.  
One April evening he found me sobbing among the moving boxes because I could no longer remember the smell of the word “bergamot.”  
He sat beside me, placed his palm on my sternum, and recited the periodic table until my breathing slowed.  
Neon, argon, krypton—elements that refuse to bond, yet glow when electrified.  
In that moment I understood that precision can also be a love language, a way of saying I see the atoms you are made of and I am not afraid.  
We did not last; narratives diverged like interstate cloverleafs.  
But I kept the taste of noble gases in my mouth, a bright ghost reminder that sometimes salvation is the absence of metaphor.

I keep returning to the compost pile behind the community garden on 21st Street.  
Nothing dramatic ever happens there—no epiphanies, no voice from the whirlwind—yet I find myself squatting beside it every equinox, turning the layers with a cracked shovel that once belonged to someone’s grandfather.  
Egg shells, coffee grounds, the limp lettuce of good intentions.  
The heap steams on cold mornings, exhaling the breath of a million micro-organisms laboring in darkness.  
I come for that exhalation, for proof that destruction can be warm rather than apocalyptic.  
Last October I buried my old journals, torn into strips like papier-mâché penance.  
I thought I was conducting an funeral, but really I was feeding the future soil where strangers will plant kale and dahlias and maybe a scraggly sunflower that reminds a child of the sun itself.  
The words will not reappear intact; they will break down into carbon and nitrogen and something unpronounceable that smells like earthworms and rain.  
This is the only afterlife I willingly believe in: not resurrection, but redistribution.

Some nights I ride the El without destination, transferring at random stations the way other people shuffle tarot decks.  
The city becomes a sentence written in neon and brake-screech, grammar provided by the conductor’s slurred vowels.  
A woman applies mascara using her phone camera, tongue pinched between teeth in concentration.  
Two teenagers negotiate a kiss like diplomats crossing hostile borders.  
A man in a Cubs cap hums “Don’t Stop Believin’” half a step flat, and the entire car becomes a choir unwillingly harmonizing around his off-key anchor.  
No one knows I am taking notes for a gospel that will never be preached.  
By the time I exit at Jarvis, the sky has lowered itself to street level, fog erasing the tops of buildings like an editor cutting exposition.  
I walk the two miles home smelling diesel and lake water, repeating the names of strangers under my breath until they dissolve into sounds, then into spit, then into nothing.

The pandemic taught us that time is not a line but a pile.  
Some days were glaciers, some were matchheads.  
I learned to measure duration by the length of my hair, by the number of bananas that ripened and rotted on the hook, by the progression of cracks across the living-room plaster.  
April 2020 felt like walking through wet cement; April 2021 felt like being the cement itself—half-set, footprinted, incapable of deciding whether to harden or collapse.  
I watched neighbors I’d never met pace their balconies in overlapping ovals, each wearing the same bewildered squint, as if the sky had been replaced by a spreadsheet they couldn’t decipher.  
One of them, an elderly man with a fedora, started hanging paper cranes on a fishing line lowered to the sidewalk.  
By August the line held hundreds, fluttering like a spine made of prayers.  
I never learned his name; maybe he never learned mine.  
The cranes turned to pulp the first hard rain, a soggy necklace no one had the heart to remove.  
The following spring, dandelions grew straight beneath the fishing line, tall enough to mimic flight.  
No moral, only manure.

I want to write a sentence long enough to wrap around the planet, a linguistic equator where nouns and verbs trade places like market vendors.  
It would start in the mouth of a newborn in Lagos and end in the dying eye of a fisherman off the coast of Alaska, detouring through every tongue and dialect, picking up slang like burrs.  
The sentence would be unreadably long, longer than payment histories, longer than statutes of limitation, longer than the pause between lightning and thunder when the storm is still deciding whether to forgive you.  
It would contain every grocery list ever lost in a reused envelope, every voicemail accidentally deleted, every name whispered into a pillow at 3 a.m. as a plea rather than an invocation.  
By the time it completed its circuit, the planet would have slowed by a millisecond, drunk on its own story.  
We would all be inside the sentence, breathing its commas, using its semicolons as tightropes.  
No one would ever reach the period; we would simply grow old mid-clause, handing the clause to our children like an heirloom lighter with no flint left.  
And the sentence would keep rolling, a Möbius strip of syntax, until language itself wore thin as church-basement carpet, until meaning and nonsense finally admitted they were conjoined twins and embraced.

But here I am, already past the 2,000-word mark, and the compost pile of my intention steams higher than I expected.  
I meant to write about the dissolution of narrative, yet I keep telling stories, because that is how we pay rent to the void: in installments of character, setting, tension, release.  
Even now, my fingers want to shape this mess into an arc—perhaps a final paragraph where I return to the amusement park and discover the photo booth has somehow printed a strip of four images after all: me at eight, my mother with her tomato, the unnamed man reciting xenon, the fedoraed neighbor folding cranes.  
We would all be smiling as if we had agreed to gather here, exiles from our own plots, finally understanding that meaning is not the bouquet we hand to the audience but the dirt we claw from beneath our nails once the curtain refuses to fall.  
I would tape the strip above my desk as a talisman against tidy endings.  
Visitors would ask who these people are, and I would shrug, offer them coffee, change the subject to the smell of earth after rain, the way the city at 4 a.m. sounds like a hard drive defragmenting itself, the rumor that tomatoes taste of copper when the person who grew them is afraid.

Instead, I will end mid-sentence, the way a subway car disappears into the tunnel before you remember whether you said goodbye, the way the compost pile tomorrow will be hotter at the center than it is today, the way stories, if they are alive at all, must consent to be eaten by something larger.  
What matters is not the moment you recognize the narrative has failed, but the instant after, when you taste the acid of the tomato, salt stinging the split on your lip, and you decide—without evidence—to swallow anyway.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
